title: Health Share Transparency Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Health Share Transparency Act of 2025 makes new rules for health care sharing ministries. These groups help people share medical costs but they are not regular insurance companies. The new law says these ministries must report their money details and how they operate to the government. They also have to tell their members clearly that the ministry might not pay for their medical bills and explain how to complain if there are problems. The government will also share reports about complaints against these ministries twice each year so people can see if there are issues.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law may be called the Health Share Transparency Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Establishing Requirements for the Disclosure of Certain Information Relating to Health Care Sharing Ministries
sections.text: This section creates new rules for health care sharing ministries. These ministries are religious organizations where members share money to help pay for each other's medical bills. Health care sharing ministries must follow several new requirements. First, they must send a report to the government at least once per year. This report must go to the Secretary of Health and Human Services, the head of the Internal Revenue Service, and the head of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. Second, they must give important information to people who want to join or who are already members. Third, they cannot work with companies to sign up new members unless those companies follow certain rules. Health care sharing ministries must report these things to the government each year. They must tell how much money they keep in reserve. They must show what percentage of member money goes to paying medical bills versus other costs. They must report how many people belong to the ministry. They must report how much money members paid in total and how much the ministry paid out for medical care. They must report the average out of pocket costs that members paid for medical care. They must list all states and counties where people can join. They must report what percentage of claims they denied. They must give contact information for the people who run the ministry. They must list all doctors and hospitals they have contracts with. They must report how long it takes on average to pay claims. The government must put all this information on a public website. Health care sharing ministries must also tell prospective and current members several important things. They must explain how to file complaints or appeal decisions, including that appeals might only go to the ministry itself. They must explain whether members must use arbitration to resolve disputes or if they can go to court. They must clearly explain that unlike real insurance, there is no guarantee the ministry will pay any claims, and they must say if there are lifetime limits on payments. They must tell members the average out of pocket costs. They must tell the average amount each member pays per year. They must compare how much the ministry paid for claims versus how much members had to pay themselves. They must list all medical services they will not pay for, and explain any conditions that would make services not payable. They must list any other requirements for claims. This information must be shared in a clear and prominent way. It must be available in multiple languages. It must be given right before someone joins. It must be written in at least fourteen point font size, or read out loud if enrollment happens over the phone. If a health care sharing ministry works with other companies to sign up members, those companies must tell potential members certain things. They must explain tax credits that might be available to buy real health insurance through government exchanges. They must explain if the person qualifies for Medicaid or Medicare. They must explain what benefits real insurance plans must provide and how cost sharing works, compared to what the ministry offers. They must explain that the ministry is not real insurance and benefits are not guaranteed. If the Secretary finds that a health care sharing ministry does not follow these rules, the Secretary can fine the ministry up to one hundred dollars per day for each person affected by the violation. The Secretary can define terms and clarify how they apply to health care sharing ministries. The Federal Trade Commission must also report on complaints about health care sharing ministries twice per year, on January first and July first. They must report how many complaints they received, what types of complaints they were, and which ministries the complaints were about, including details about who owns and runs those ministries. This reporting requirement starts ninety days after this law is passed.
